lifecycle_phases.name: connection_establishment
lifecycle_phases.snippet: The 'three-way handshake' is the procedure used to establish a connection... initiated by one TCP peer and responded to by another.
lifecycle_phases.rationale: Section 3.5 and 3.10.1 describe the structured initiation sequence (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) and state transitions from CLOSED/LISTEN through SYN-SENT/SYN-RECEIVED to ESTABLISHED.

lifecycle_phases.name: data_transfer
lifecycle_phases.snippet: Once the connection is established, data is communicated by the exchange of segments.
lifecycle_phases.rationale: Section 3.8 and the SEND/RECEIVE call processing in 3.10.2–3.10.3 define the data-transfer phase where SND.NXT, RCV.NXT, and SND.UNA are advanced.

lifecycle_phases.name: connection_closing
lifecycle_phases.snippet: CLOSE is an operation meaning 'I have no more data to send.'... a FIN segment can be constructed and placed on the outgoing segment queue.
lifecycle_phases.rationale: Section 3.6 and 3.10.4 describe the FIN-based shutdown handshake, half-closed connections, and TIME-WAIT lingering.

lifecycle_phases.name: connection_reset_abort
lifecycle_phases.snippet: A TCP user or application can issue a reset on a connection at any time, though reset events are also generated by the protocol itself when various error conditions occur.
lifecycle_phases.rationale: Sections 3.5.1–3.5.3 and 3.10.5 cover error-triggered and application-triggered connection teardown via RST, a distinct lifecycle phase from graceful close.

lifecycle_phases.name: quiet_time_after_reboot
lifecycle_phases.snippet: Hosts that for any reason lose knowledge of the last sequence numbers transmitted on each active connection shall delay emitting any TCP segments for at least the agreed MSL.
lifecycle_phases.rationale: Sections 3.4.2–3.4.3 define a pre-operational constraint phase requiring silence for one MSL after state loss, separable from normal establishment.

state_machine_candidates.name: tcp_connection_state_diagram
state_machine_candidates.snippet: A TCP connection progresses from one state to another in response to events. The events are the user calls, OPEN, SEND, RECEIVE, CLOSE, ABORT, and STATUS; the incoming segments... and timeouts.
state_machine_candidates.rationale: Section 3.3.2 and Figure 5 explicitly enumerate all states and the event-driven transitions between them; this is the canonical FSM reference in the document.

state_machine_candidates.name: closed_state
state_machine_candidates.snippet: CLOSED – represents no connection state at all.
state_machine_candidates.rationale: CLOSED is listed as a distinct (fictional) state in 3.3.2 and is referenced throughout event-processing sections as the initial and terminal condition.

state_machine_candidates.name: listen_state
state_machine_candidates.snippet: LISTEN – represents waiting for a connection request from any remote TCP peer and port.
state_machine_candidates.rationale: Named state in 3.3.2 and detailed segment-arrival processing in 3.10.7.2.

state_machine_candidates.name: syn_sent_state
state_machine_candidates.snippet: SYN-SENT – represents waiting for a matching connection request after having sent a connection request.
state_machine_candidates.rationale: Named state in 3.3.2; detailed processing in 3.10.7.3.

state_machine_candidates.name: syn_received_state
state_machine_candidates.snippet: SYN-RECEIVED – represents waiting for a confirming connection request acknowledgment after having both received and sent a connection request.
state_machine_candidates.rationale: Named state in 3.3.2; ACK processing in 3.10.7.4 triggers ESTABLISHED transition.

state_machine_candidates.name: established_state
state_machine_candidates.snippet: ESTABLISHED – represents an open connection, data received can be delivered to the user. The normal state for the data transfer phase.
state_machine_candidates.rationale: Named state in 3.3.2; central state for data transfer processing in sections 3.8 and 3.10.7.4.

state_machine_candidates.name: fin_wait_1_state
state_machine_candidates.snippet: FIN-WAIT-1 – represents waiting for a connection termination request from the remote TCP peer, or an acknowledgment of the connection termination request previously sent.
state_machine_candidates.rationale: Named state in 3.3.2; triggered by CLOSE call from ESTABLISHED; transitions to FIN-WAIT-2 or TIME-WAIT on ACK/FIN events.

state_machine_candidates.name: fin_wait_2_state
state_machine_candidates.snippet: FIN-WAIT-2 – represents waiting for a connection termination request from the remote TCP peer.
state_machine_candidates.rationale: Named state in 3.3.2; transitions to TIME-WAIT on receiving FIN.

state_machine_candidates.name: close_wait_state
state_machine_candidates.snippet: CLOSE-WAIT – represents waiting for a connection termination request from the local user.
state_machine_candidates.rationale: Named state in 3.3.2; entered when remote FIN is received; RECEIVE call processing specifically handles this state.

state_machine_candidates.name: closing_state
state_machine_candidates.snippet: CLOSING – represents waiting for a connection termination request acknowledgment from the remote TCP peer.
state_machine_candidates.rationale: Named state in 3.3.2; arises from simultaneous close; transitions to TIME-WAIT when FIN is ACKed.

state_machine_candidates.name: last_ack_state
state_machine_candidates.snippet: LAST-ACK – represents waiting for an acknowledgment of the connection termination request previously sent to the remote TCP peer.
state_machine_candidates.rationale: Named state in 3.3.2; terminal state before CLOSED; ACK causes TCB deletion.

state_machine_candidates.name: time_wait_state
state_machine_candidates.snippet: TIME-WAIT – represents waiting for enough time to pass to be sure the remote TCP peer received the acknowledgment of its connection termination request.
state_machine_candidates.rationale: Named state in 3.3.2; must linger for 2xMSL (MUST-13); TIME-WAIT TIMEOUT causes TCB deletion.

message_structure_candidates.name: tcp_header_format
message_structure_candidates.snippet: A TCP header, followed by any user data in the segment, is formatted as follows...
message_structure_candidates.rationale: Section 3.1 provides the canonical TCP header diagram (Figure 1) with all named fields: Source Port, Destination Port, Sequence Number, Acknowledgment Number, Data Offset, Reserved, Control bits, Window, Checksum, Urgent Pointer, Options, Data.

message_structure_candidates.name: ipv4_pseudo_header
message_structure_candidates.snippet: The checksum also covers a pseudo-header (Figure 2) conceptually prefixed to the TCP header. The pseudo-header is 96 bits for IPv4 and 320 bits for IPv6.
message_structure_candidates.rationale: Section 3.1 defines the pseudo-header structure required for checksum computation, a distinct message-structure artifact from the TCP header itself.

message_structure_candidates.name: mandatory_option_set
message_structure_candidates.snippet: Kind 0: End of Option List Option. Kind 1: No-Operation. Kind 2 (length 4): Maximum Segment Size.
message_structure_candidates.rationale: Table 1 in Section 3.1 lists the three TCP options that MUST be supported (MUST-4), forming a minimal mandatory message-structure vocabulary.

message_structure_candidates.name: tcp_options_format
message_structure_candidates.snippet: Case 1: A single octet of option-kind. Case 2: An octet of option-kind (Kind), an octet of option-length, and the actual option-data octets.
message_structure_candidates.rationale: Section 3.1 and 3.2 define the two-case option format and specific option encodings; implementations must handle unknown options with a length field.

message_structure_candidates.name: common_options
message_structure_candidates.snippet: Additional RFCs define some other commonly used options... TCP Selective Acknowledgment (SACK) Option, TCP Timestamp (TS) Option, and TCP Window Scale (WS) Option.
message_structure_candidates.rationale: Section 3.2.1 names three recommended high-performance options by surface form; they are referenced but fully defined in external RFCs.

message_structure_candidates.name: syn_segment
message_structure_candidates.snippet: Issue a SYN segment. An initial send sequence number (ISS) is selected. A SYN segment of the form <seq=iss><ctl=syn> is sent.
message_structure_candidates.rationale: SYN segments appear in handshake examples (Figures 6–8) and OPEN call processing with defined sequence number and control field values.

message_structure_candidates.name: syn_ack_segment
message_structure_candidates.snippet: ISS should be selected and a SYN segment sent of the form: <seq=iss><ack=rcv.nxt><ctl=syn,ack>
message_structure_candidates.rationale: The SYN-ACK is the second message in the three-way handshake, sent by the LISTEN-state peer; appears in handshake figures and LISTEN state processing.

message_structure_candidates.name: rst_segment
message_structure_candidates.snippet: As a general rule, reset (RST) is sent whenever a segment arrives that apparently is not intended for the current connection.
message_structure_candidates.rationale: Sections 3.5.2–3.5.3 define RST generation and processing rules; RST appears throughout event-processing sections with specific sequence/ACK field construction rules.

message_structure_candidates.name: fin_segment
message_structure_candidates.snippet: a FIN segment can be constructed and placed on the outgoing segment queue. No further SENDs from the user will be accepted.
message_structure_candidates.rationale: FIN is the protocol message for graceful connection termination; appears in closing sequence diagrams (Figures 12–13) and FIN-bit processing in 3.10.7.4.

message_structure_candidates.name: ack_segment
message_structure_candidates.snippet: Send an acknowledgment of the form: <seq=snd.nxt><ack=rcv.nxt><ctl=ack>. This acknowledgment should be piggybacked on a segment being transmitted if possible.
message_structure_candidates.rationale: ACK segments are the primary feedback mechanism; Section 3.8.6.3 and 3.10.7.4 define when and how to generate them.

timer_candidates.name: retransmission_timeout
timer_candidates.snippet: the retransmission timeout (RTO) must be dynamically determined. The RTO MUST be computed according to the algorithm in [10], including Karn's algorithm for taking RTT samples (MUST-18).
timer_candidates.rationale: Section 3.8.1 mandates dynamic RTO computation; Section 3.10.8 defines RETRANSMISSION TIMEOUT event processing: resend front of retransmission queue and reinitialize timer.

timer_candidates.name: time_wait_timeout
timer_candidates.snippet: When a connection is closed actively, it MUST linger in the TIME-WAIT state for a time 2xMSL (Maximum Segment Lifetime) (MUST-13).
timer_candidates.rationale: Section 3.10.8 explicitly names TIME-WAIT TIMEOUT as a discrete event; Section 3.6.1 specifies the 2xMSL duration requirement.

timer_candidates.name: user_timeout
timer_candidates.snippet: For any state if the user timeout expires, flush all queues, signal the user 'error: connection aborted due to user timeout' in general and for any outstanding calls, delete the TCB, enter the CLOSED state.
timer_candidates.rationale: Section 3.10.8 defines USER TIMEOUT as the third discrete timeout event; Section 3.8.3 defines R1/R2 thresholds that govern when this fires.

timer_candidates.name: keep_alive_timer
timer_candidates.snippet: Keep-alive packets MUST only be sent when no sent data is outstanding, and no data or acknowledgment packets have been received for the connection within an interval (MUST-26). This interval MUST be configurable (MUST-27) and MUST default to no less than two hours (MUST-28).
timer_candidates.rationale: Section 3.8.4 describes the keep-alive probe mechanism with a configurable timer defaulting to ≥2 hours; it is a distinct optional timer.

timer_candidates.name: zero_window_probe_timer
timer_candidates.snippet: The transmitting host SHOULD send the first zero-window probe when a zero window has existed for the retransmission timeout period (SHLD-29), and SHOULD increase exponentially the interval between successive probes (SHLD-30).
timer_candidates.rationale: Section 3.8.6.1 defines ZWP probing triggered after the RTO period with exponential back-off; Section 3.8.6.2.1 mentions a related SWS override timeout (0.1–1.0 s).

timer_candidates.name: delayed_ack_timer
timer_candidates.snippet: the delay MUST be less than 0.5 seconds (MUST-40). An ACK SHOULD be generated for at least every second full-sized segment.
timer_candidates.rationale: Section 3.8.6.3 defines a bounded delay timer for ACK aggregation; the 0.5 s upper bound is a MUST-level constraint.

symbol_candidates.name: snd_una
symbol_candidates.rationale: Key send-sequence state variable in Table 2 (Section 3.3.1); used in acceptability tests, ACK processing, and window computations throughout Sections 3.4, 3.8, 3.10.

symbol_candidates.name: snd_nxt
symbol_candidates.snippet: SND.NXT = next sequence number to be sent
symbol_candidates.rationale: Core send variable in Table 2; advanced on every transmitted segment; appears in almost every event-processing rule.

symbol_candidates.name: snd_wnd
symbol_candidates.rationale: Key flow-control variable; its value governs when new data can be sent; appears in usable-window formula U = SND.UNA + SND.WND - SND.NXT.

symbol_candidates.name: rcv_nxt
symbol_candidates.snippet: RCV.NXT = next sequence number expected on an incoming segment, and is the left or lower edge of the receive window
symbol_candidates.rationale: Core receive variable in Table 3; advanced on accepted segments; defines the left edge of the receive window in acceptability tests.

symbol_candidates.name: rcv_wnd
symbol_candidates.rationale: Advertised receive window variable in Table 3; central to window management, SWS avoidance, and zero-window probing.

symbol_candidates.name: iss
symbol_candidates.snippet: ISS – initial send sequence number... TCP implementations SHOULD generate its initial sequence numbers with the expression: ISN = M + F(localip, localport, remoteip, remoteport, secretkey)
symbol_candidates.rationale: ISS is selected at connection open and anchors the SYN segment sequence space; its secure generation (MUST-8, SHLD-1) is a critical protocol requirement.

symbol_candidates.name: irs
symbol_candidates.rationale: IRS is learned during the three-way handshake and anchors the receive sequence space; listed in Table 3 (Section 3.3.1).

symbol_candidates.name: seg_seq
symbol_candidates.rationale: Per-segment variable in Table 4; used in all acceptability tests and RST/ACK field constructions.

symbol_candidates.name: seg_ack
symbol_candidates.rationale: Per-segment variable in Table 4; compared against SND.UNA/SND.NXT for acceptable ACK determination.

symbol_candidates.name: seg_wnd
symbol_candidates.rationale: Per-segment variable in Table 4; used to update SND.WND in established-state ACK processing.

symbol_candidates.name: control_bits_flags
symbol_candidates.snippet: The currently assigned control bits are CWR, ECE, URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, and FIN.
symbol_candidates.rationale: Section 3.1 defines all 8 control bits by name and 1-bit size; they are the primary event discriminators in all state-machine processing rules.

symbol_candidates.name: msl
symbol_candidates.snippet: MSL – Maximum Segment Lifetime, the time a TCP segment can exist in the internetwork system. Arbitrarily defined to be 2 minutes.
symbol_candidates.rationale: MSL is the fundamental time constant for TIME-WAIT duration (2xMSL), quiet time, and ISN cycle reasoning.

symbol_candidates.name: urg_urgent_pointer
symbol_candidates.snippet: The urgent pointer points to the sequence number of the octet following the urgent data. This field is only to be interpreted in segments with the URG control bit set.
symbol_candidates.rationale: Section 3.1 defines the Urgent Pointer field; Section 3.8.5 defines the urgent mechanism including urgent mode transitions signaled to the application.

symbol_candidates.name: r1_r2_thresholds
symbol_candidates.snippet: There are two thresholds R1 and R2 measuring the amount of retransmission that has occurred for the same segment... When transmissions reach R2, close the connection.
symbol_candidates.rationale: Section 3.8.3 defines R1/R2 as mandatory thresholds governing connection failure detection; R2 must be configurable by the application (MUST-21).

symbol_candidates.name: tcb
symbol_candidates.snippet: We conceive of these variables being stored in a connection record called a Transmission Control Block or TCB.
symbol_candidates.rationale: The TCB is the runtime data structure holding all per-connection state; referenced in every OPEN, CLOSE, ABORT, and STATUS call processing rule.

symbol_candidates.name: eff_snd_mss
symbol_candidates.rationale: Section 3.7.1 defines the effective send MSS formula; used in SWS avoidance and Nagle algorithm decisions.

symbol_candidates.name: isn_generation_formula
surface_forms: ISN = M + F(localip, localport, remoteip, remoteport, secretkey)
symbol_candidates.snippet: ISN = M + F(localip, localport, remoteip, remoteport, secretkey) where M is the 4 microsecond timer, and F() is a pseudorandom function (PRF).
symbol_candidates.rationale: Section 3.4.1 provides the normative ISN generation expression with a secret-key PRF; F() MUST NOT be computable from outside (MUST-9).

runtime_dependency_candidates.name: retransmission_queue
runtime_dependency_candidates.snippet: pointers to the retransmit queue and to the current segment... send the segment at the front of the retransmission queue again, reinitialize the retransmission timer.
runtime_dependency_candidates.rationale: The retransmission queue is a mandatory runtime buffer referenced in the TCB definition and retransmission timeout event; its management is central to reliable delivery.

runtime_dependency_candidates.name: send_receive_buffers
runtime_dependency_candidates.snippet: pointers to the user's send and receive buffers... RCV.BUFF is the total receive buffer space.
runtime_dependency_candidates.rationale: The TCB definition in 3.3.1 requires buffer pointers; SWS avoidance and ZWP algorithms depend on RCV.BUFF as a runtime quantity.

runtime_dependency_candidates.name: checksum_computation
runtime_dependency_candidates.snippet: The TCP checksum is never optional. The sender MUST generate it (MUST-2) and the receiver MUST check it (MUST-3).
runtime_dependency_candidates.rationale: The checksum covering header, data, and pseudo-header is a mandatory runtime computation on every send and receive path.

runtime_dependency_candidates.name: ip_layer_interface
runtime_dependency_candidates.snippet: For the case that the lower level is IP, we note some of the parameter values that TCP implementations might use.
runtime_dependency_candidates.rationale: Section 3.9.2 defines runtime dependencies on the IP layer for segment delivery, TTL configuration, ICMP error reception, and Diffserv field setting.

runtime_dependency_candidates.name: icmp_error_handling
runtime_dependency_candidates.snippet: ICMP error messages must be handled... distinguishing between soft and hard errors; mandates the silent discarding of Source Quench messages.
runtime_dependency_candidates.rationale: Section 3.9.2.2 requires TCP to process ICMP messages from the IP layer at runtime to detect path failures and unreachable destinations.

runtime_dependency_candidates.name: congestion_control_algorithms
runtime_dependency_candidates.snippet: A TCP endpoint MUST implement the basic congestion control algorithms slow start, congestion avoidance, and exponential backoff of RTO to avoid creating congestion collapse conditions (MUST-19).
runtime_dependency_candidates.rationale: Section 3.8.2 mandates runtime congestion control as an environmental dependency; delegates algorithm details to RFC 5681 and RFC 6298.

runtime_dependency_candidates.name: path_mtu_discovery
runtime_dependency_candidates.snippet: implementation of Path MTU Discovery (PMTUD) and Packetization Layer Path MTU Discovery (PLPMTUD) is strongly recommended in order for TCP to improve segmentation decisions.
runtime_dependency_candidates.rationale: Section 3.7.2 defines a runtime dependency on PMTUD/PLPMTUD to dynamically determine maximum segment size; interacts with IP and ICMP layers.

runtime_dependency_candidates.name: nagle_algorithm
runtime_dependency_candidates.snippet: If there is unacknowledged data (i.e., SND.NXT > SND.UNA), then the sending TCP endpoint buffers all user data... until the outstanding data has been acknowledged or until TCP can send a full-sized segment.
runtime_dependency_candidates.rationale: Section 3.7.4 defines the Nagle algorithm as a SHOULD-level runtime mechanism with a mandatory disable option (MUST-17); it interacts with SWS avoidance.

runtime_dependency_candidates.name: user_tcp_interface_calls
runtime_dependency_candidates.snippet: The following functional description of user commands to the TCP implementation is, at best, fictional, since every operating system will have different facilities... all TCP implementations must provide a minimum set of services.
runtime_dependency_candidates.rationale: Section 3.9.1 defines the application-layer call interface as a runtime dependency boundary; the seven defined calls trigger all protocol events.

runtime_dependency_candidates.name: source_address_validation
runtime_dependency_candidates.snippet: security requirements for source address validation, specifically regarding the handling of invalid, broadcast, or multicast addresses in incoming segments.
runtime_dependency_candidates.rationale: Section 3.9.2.3 mandates runtime validation of source addresses on incoming SYN segments, a security-related environmental dependency.

recommended_merge_groups.name: state_machine_core
recommended_merge_groups.reason: Section 3.3.2 (state definitions + Figure 5) and Section 3.10 (SEGMENT ARRIVES + Timeouts) together constitute the complete FSM; they must be read jointly to map every state × event → action × next-state.

recommended_merge_groups.name: connection_establishment_group
recommended_merge_groups.reason: ISN selection (3.4.1), three-way handshake (3.5), half-open/RST handling (3.5.1–3.5.3), and OPEN call event processing (3.10.1) together define all establishment paths including simultaneous open and error recovery.

recommended_merge_groups.name: connection_teardown_group
recommended_merge_groups.reason: Closing (3.6), half-closed connections (3.6.1), CLOSE/ABORT calls (3.10.4–3.10.5), FIN-bit processing in 3.10.7.4, and TIME-WAIT TIMEOUT (3.10.8) must be read together to trace all teardown paths.

recommended_merge_groups.name: tcp_header_and_options
recommended_merge_groups.reason: Header format (3.1) and option definitions (3.2) are interdependent; field sizes, option encoding rules, and mandatory option table form a single message-structure unit.

recommended_merge_groups.name: sequence_number_and_window_management
recommended_merge_groups.reason: TCB send/receive variables (3.3.1), sequence acceptability tests (3.4), data communication (3.8), window management (3.8.6), ZWP (3.8.6.1), and SWS avoidance (3.8.6.2) are tightly coupled and reference the same variables.

recommended_merge_groups.name: retransmission_and_congestion
recommended_merge_groups.reason: RTO computation (3.8.1), congestion control requirements (3.8.2), connection failure thresholds R1/R2 (3.8.3), and RETRANSMISSION TIMEOUT event (3.10.8) are operationally coupled and must be extracted together.

recommended_merge_groups.name: isn_security_and_quiet_time
recommended_merge_groups.reason: ISN selection formula (3.4.1), quiet time concept (3.4.2–3.4.3), and MSL definition interact to define the security and uniqueness guarantees on connection identifiers.

recommended_merge_groups.name: urgent_mechanism
recommended_merge_groups.reason: Urgent information description (3.8.5) and URG-bit processing in 3.10.7.4 together define the complete urgent data path including mode transitions and application notification.

ambiguities.description: Figure 5 (TCP Connection State Diagram) is presented as a summary with explicit disclaimer 'must not be taken as the total specification. Many details are not included.' Transitions involving RST from any state to TIME-WAIT/CLOSED are omitted from the figure for legibility.
ambiguities.suggested_followup: Audit Section 3.10.7.4 (Other States) RST-bit processing and Section 3.5.2 (Reset Generation) to reconstruct the full RST-triggered transition table not shown in Figure 5.

ambiguities.description: TIME-WAIT reuse with Timestamp Options is described as an improvement (referencing RFC 6191 [40]) that supersedes the standard sequence-number-based check, but the full algorithm is defined in an external RFC, not reproduced here.
ambiguities.suggested_followup: Extraction of TIME-WAIT reuse logic must note the external RFC 6191 dependency; the in-document text only states applicability conditions, not the algorithm itself.

ambiguities.description: The SWS override timeout (0.1–1.0 seconds range) in Section 3.8.6.2.1 is described as possibly combinable with the ZWP timer (Section 3.8.6.1), creating ambiguity about whether these are one timer or two.
ambiguities.suggested_followup: Audit whether implementations treat the SWS override and ZWP probe timer as a single shared timer or separate timers; the text says 'may be convenient to combine' but does not mandate it.

ambiguities.description: The 'PUSH' flag (PSH bit) behavior is described informally in multiple places (SEND call, data delivery, FIN implies PUSH) but its end-to-end semantics are noted as implementation-dependent; Appendix B.TCP Requirement Summary contains a table summarizing PUSH requirements.
ambiguities.suggested_followup: Cross-check PSH bit requirements across Section 3.9.1.2 (SEND Call), 3.10.7.4 (segment text processing), and Appendix B table to resolve whether PSH delivery to the application is mandatory or advisory.

ambiguities.description: RFC 5961 mitigations (challenge ACK for RST and SYN in synchronized states, ACK acceptability window) are described as optional alternatives to original RFC 793 behavior; both paths appear in Section 3.10.7.4 with conditional language, creating dual-track processing logic.
ambiguities.suggested_followup: Determine which RFC 5961 mitigations the target implementation adopts, as this changes which processing branches apply for RST (step 2) and SYN (step 4) in synchronized states.

ambiguities.description: The 'Experimental TCP Options' (Section 3.2.2) and 'Other Common Options' (3.2.1) are referenced by RFC number only; their option-kind values, formats, and behavioral interactions are not defined within this document.
ambiguities.suggested_followup: If SACK, Timestamps, or Window Scale are relevant to the extraction target, their definitions must be sourced from RFC 2018, RFC 7323, etc., not from RFC 9293 itself.

ambiguities.description: Security and privacy considerations (Section 7) reference IPsec, TCP-AO, and TLS as external mechanisms but note TCP has no built-in cryptographic protection; the boundary between protocol-native security (RST validation, ISN randomization) and externally-provided security is not sharply delineated.
ambiguities.suggested_followup: Audit Section 7 alongside Section 3.4.1 (ISN PRF), Section 3.5.3 (RST validation), and Section 3.9.2.3 (source address validation) to map which security properties are protocol-intrinsic versus externally delegated.
